volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Pot of Gold (Section 3.5.530 - Section 4.4.637b)

sections.label: Section 3.5.530
sections.original: etiam ipsus ultro debet argentario :
sections.translation: even already is the husband himself in debt to the banker , and the hopes of the tax-gatlherer are postponed to another day . These , and many others , are the inconveniences and intolerable expenses of great portions ; but she who is without portion is in the power of her husband ;

sections.label: Section 3.5.535
sections.original: dotatae mactant et malo et damno viros .
sections.translation: the portioned ones overwhelm their husbands with loss and ruin . But see ; here ’ s my connexion by marriage before the house ! How do you do , Euclio ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.537
sections.original: Nimium lubenter edi sermonem tuom .
sections.translation: With very great pleasure have I listened to your discourse .

sections.label: Section 3.6.538
sections.original: An audivisti ?
sections.translation: Did you hear me ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.538b
sections.original: Vsque a principio omnia .
sections.translation: Everything from the very beginning .

sections.label: Section 3.6.539
sections.original: Tamen meó quidem animo aliquánto facias rectius ,
sections.translation: Still , in my way of thinking indeed , you would be acting a little more becomingly

sections.label: Section 3.6.540
sections.original: si nitidior sis filiai nuptiis .
sections.translation: if you were more tidy at the wedding of your daughter .

sections.label: Line 541
sections.original: Pro re nitorem et gloriam pro copia
sections.translation: Those who have display according to their circumstances and splendour according to their means [*] , remember themselves , from whence they are sprung ; neither by myself , Megadorus , nor by any poor man , are better circumstances enjoyed than appearances warrant .

sections.label: Section 3.6.545
sections.original: Immo est quod satis est , et di faciant ut siet
sections.translation: Surely they are ; and may the Gods , I hope , make them so to be , and more and more may they prosper that which you now possess .

sections.label: Section 3.6.547
sections.original: Illud mihi verbum non placet quod nunc habes .
sections.translation: That expression don ’ t please me , which you now possess . He knows that I ’ ve got this , as well as I do myself : the old woman has discovered it to him .

sections.label: Section 3.6.549
sections.original: Quid tu te solus e senatu sevocas ?
sections.translation: Why do you separate yourself thus alone , apart from the Senate [*] ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.550
sections.original: Pol ego ut te accusem merito meditabar .
sections.translation: Troth , I was considering whether I should accuse you deservedly .

sections.label: Section 3.6.550b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.551
sections.original: Quid sit me rogitas ? qui mihi omnis angulos
sections.translation: Do you ask me what ’ s the matter ? You who have filled every corner in my house , for wretched me , with thieves ? You who have introduced into my dwelling five hundred cooks , with six hands a-piece , of the race of Geryon [*] ,

sections.label: Section 3.6.555
sections.original: quos si Argus servet , qui oculeus totus fuit ,
sections.translation: whom were Argus to watch , who was eyes all over , that Juno once set as a spy upon Jupiter , he never could watch them ; a music-girl besides , who could alone drink up for me the Corinthian fountain of Pirene [*] , if it were flowing with wine ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.560
sections.original: tum obsonium autem —
sections.translation: And then as to provisions —

sections.label: Section 3.6.560b
sections.original: Pol vel legioni sat est .
sections.translation: Troth , there ’ s enough for a procurer [*] even . I sent as much as a lamb .

sections.label: Section 3.6.561b
sections.original: Quo quidem agno sat scio
sections.translation: Than which lamb , I , indeed , know right well that there is nowhere a more curious [*] beast existing .

sections.label: Section 3.6.563
sections.original: Volo ego ex te scire qui sit agnus curio .
sections.translation: I wish to know of you why is this lamb curious ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.564
sections.original: Quia ossa ac pellis totust , ita cura macet .
sections.translation: Because it ’ s all skin and bone , so lean is it with care ;

sections.label: Section 3.6.565
sections.original: quin exta inspicere in sole ei vivo licet :
sections.translation: why , even when alive , by the light of the sun you may look at its entrails ; it ’ s just as transparent as a Punic lantern [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.6.567
sections.original: Caedundum conduxi égo illum .
sections.translation: I bought it to be killed .

sections.label: Section 3.6.567b
sections.original: Tum tu idem optumumst
sections.translation: Then it ’ s best that you likewise should bargain for [*] it to be carried out for burial ; for I believe it ’ s dead by this time .

sections.label: Section 3.6.569
sections.original: Potare ego hodie , Euclio , tecum volo .
sections.translation: Euclio , I wish this day to have a drinking with you .

sections.label: Section 3.6.570
sections.original: Non potem égo quidem hercle .
sections.translation: By my troth , I really must not drink .

sections.label: Section 3.6.570b
sections.original: At ego iussero
sections.translation: But I ’ ll order one cask of old wine to be brought from my house .

sections.label: Section 3.6.572
sections.original: Nolo hercle , nam mihi bibere decretum est aquam .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I won ’ t have it ; for I ’ ve determined to drink water .

sections.label: Section 3.6.573
sections.original: Ego te hodie reddam madidum , si vivo , probe ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll have you well drenched this day , if I live , you who have determined to drink water .

sections.label: Section 3.6.574b
sections.original: Scio quam rem agat :
sections.translation: I know what plan he ’ s upon ;

sections.label: Section 3.6.575
sections.original: ut me deponat vino , eam adfectat viam ,
sections.translation: he ’ s aiming at this method , to overcome me with wine , and after that , to change the settlement [*] of what I possess : I ’ ll take care of that , for I ’ ll hide it somewhere out of doors . I ’ ll make him lose his wine and his trouble together .

sections.label: Section 3.6.579
sections.original: Ego , nisi quid me vis , eo lavatum , ut sacruficem .—
sections.translation: Unless you want me for anything , I ’ m going to bathe , that I may sacrifice .

sections.label: Section 3.6.580
sections.original: Edepol , ne tu , aula , multos inimicos habes
sections.translation: By my faith , you pot ( taking it from under his cloak ) , you surely have many enemies , and that gold as well which is entrusted to you ! Now this is the best thing to be done by me , to take you away , my pot , to the Temple of Faith [*] , where I ’ ll hide you carefully . Faith , thou dost know me , and I thee ; please , do have a care

sections.label: Section 3.6.585
sections.original: ne in me mutassis nomen , si hoc concreduo .
sections.translation: not to change thy name against me , if I entrust this to thee . Faith , I ’ ll come to thee , relying on thy fidelity .

sections.label: Section 4.1.587
sections.original: Hóc est servi facinus frugi , facere quod ego persequor ,
sections.translation: This is the duty of a good servant , to do what I ’ m intending , not to consider the commands of his master a bore or trouble to him . For that servant who resolves to serve his master with hearty goodwill ,

sections.label: Section 4.1.590
sections.original: in erum matura , in se sera condecet capessere .
sections.translation: him it behoves to act expeditiously for his master , slowly for himself ; but if he sleeps , let him so sleep as to bethink himself that he is a servant . But he who lives in servitude to one in love , as I am serving , if he sees love overcoming his master , this I think to be the duty of the servant ; to restrain him for his safety , not to impel him onwards towards his own inclination .

sections.label: Section 4.1.595
sections.original: quasi pueri qui nare discunt scirpea induitur ratis ,
sections.translation: Just as a float of bulrushes is placed beneath boys who are learning to swim , by means of which they may labour less , so as to swim more easily and move their hands ; in the same way do I consider that it is proper for the servant to be a buoy to his master thus in love , so as to bear him up lest he should go to the bottom ; and so ⋯ should he learn the will of his master , that his eyes should know what his mouth chooses not to speak .

sections.label: Section 4.1.600
sections.original: quod iubeat citis quadrigis citius properet persequi .
sections.translation: What he orders , he should hasten to perform more swiftly than the swift steeds . He who shall have a care for these things , will escape the castigation of the ox ’ s hide , nor by his own means will he ever bring the fetters to brightness . Now , my master ’ s in love with the daughter of this poor man , Euclio ; word has just now been brought to my master that she is given to Megadorus here :

sections.label: Section 4.1.605
sections.original: is speculatum huc misit me , ut quae fierent fieret particeps .
sections.translation: he has sent me here to spy out , that he may be made acquainted with the things that are going on . Now , without any suspicion , I ’ ll sit here by the sacred altar [*] . From this spot I shall be able , in this direction and that , to witness what they are about .

sections.label: Section 4.2.608
sections.original: Tu modo cave quoiquam indicassis aurum meum esse istic , Fides :
sections.translation: O Goddess Faith , do thou but take care not to discover to any person that my gold is there . I have no fear that any one will find it , so well is it concealed in its hiding place .

sections.label: Section 4.2.610
sections.original: edepol ne illic pulchram praedam agát , si quis illam invenerit
sections.translation: By my troth , he will surely have a charming booty there , if any one shall meet with that pot loaded with gold . But I entreat thee , Faith , to hinder that . Now I shall go wash me , that I may perform the sacrifice ; so that I may not delay my new connexion by marriage , but that , when he sends to me , he may forthwith take my daughter home . Over and over again now , Goddess Faith , do thou take care that I shall carry away the pot safe from thy Temple .

sections.label: Section 4.2.615
sections.original: tuae fide concredidi aurum , in tuo luco et fano est situm .—
sections.translation: To thy fidelity [*] have I entrusted the gold ; in thy grove and Temple is it placed .

sections.label: Section 4.2.616
sections.original: Di immortales , quod ego hunc hominem facinus audivi loqui :
sections.translation: Immortal Gods , what a deed did I hear this person speaking of , how that he had hidden here , in the Temple of Faith , a pot filled with gold ; prithee , beware you , how you are more faithful to him than to myself ! And he , as I fancy , is the father of her whom my master ’ s in love with .

sections.label: Section 4.2.620
sections.original: ibo hinc intro , perscrutabor fanum , si inveniam uspiam
sections.translation: I ’ ll go hence into it ; I ’ ll thoroughly ransack the Temple , to see if I can anywhere find the gold , while he ’ s engaged . But if I do find it , O Goddess Faith , I ’ ll offer to thee a gallon jug [*] full of honeyed wine , that I ’ ll surely offer to thee ; but I ’ ll drink it up myself , when I have offered it .

sections.label: Section 4.3.624
sections.original: Non temere est quod corvos cantat mihi nunc ab laeva manu ;
sections.translation: It wasn ’ t for nothing that the raven was just now croaking on my left hand [*] ;

sections.label: Section 4.3.625
sections.original: semul radebat pedibus terram et voce croccibat sua :
sections.translation: he was both scratching the ground with his feet and croaking with his voice . At once my heart began to jump about [*] , and to leap within my breast . But why do I delay to run ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.628
sections.original: I foras , lumbrice , qui sub terra erepsisti modo ,
sections.translation: Out , out , you earthworm [*] , who have this instant crept out of the earth ; who just now were nowhere seen , and now that you are seen shall die for it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.630
sections.original: ego pol te , praestrigiator , miseris iam accipiam modis .
sections.translation: By my faith , you juggler , I ’ ll receive you now after a disagreable fashion .

sections.label: Section 4.4.631
sections.original: Quae te mala crux agitat ? quid tibi mecum est commerci , senex ?
sections.translation: What the curst plague does ail you ? What business have you with me , old fellow ? Why do you torment me ? Why are you dragging me ? For what reason are you beating me ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.633
sections.original: Verberabilissime , etiam rogitas , non fur , sed trifur ?
sections.translation: You out-and-out whipping-post , do you even ask that , you , not thief , but thrice-dotted thief .

sections.label: Section 4.4.634
sections.original: Quid tibi súrrupui ?
sections.translation: What have I stolen from you ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.634b
sections.original: Redde huc sis .
sections.translation: Give me that back here , if you please .

sections.label: Section 4.4.634c
sections.original: Quid tibi vis reddam ?
sections.translation: What do you want me to give you back ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.634d
sections.original: Rogas ?
sections.translation: Do you ask me that ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.635
sections.original: Nil equidem tibi abstuli .
sections.translation: As for me , I ’ ve taken nothing away from you .

sections.label: Section 4.4.635b
sections.original: At illud quod tibi abstuleras cedo .
sections.translation: But give up that which you have taken away for yourself . Are you going to do so ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.636b
sections.original: Quid agám ?
sections.translation: Do what ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.636c
sections.original: Auferre non potes .
sections.translation: You can ’ t carry it off .

sections.label: Section 4.4.636d
sections.original: Quid vis tibi ?
sections.translation: What do you want ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.637
sections.original: Pone .
sections.translation: Lay it down .

sections.label: Section 4.4.637b
sections.original: Id quidem pol te datare credo consuetum , senex .
sections.translation: Troth , for my part , I think that you are in the habit [*] of quizzing , old gentleman .
